intro: Technical calculations are essential in printing and design: choosing the right DPI ensures print quality and prevents wasted file size. Calculating ideal pixel resolution for a given print size helps designers, photographers, and print shops meet specifications. This calculator applies the standard formula with your inputs, checking typical ranges and alerting you to unusual values. Values updated to 2026 and reviewed periodically to keep you current.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **Pixels = inches × DPI**. Apply this directly with your input values to find the ideal resolution for any print size.

explanation:
Understanding DPI and pixel resolution is fundamental for print professionals, designers, and photographers. This calculator helps you determine the right pixel dimensions for any print project.

## How to Calculate

This calculator applies the standard DPI formula to your dimensions. Enter your desired print size in centimeters and select your target DPI, and the calculator will compute the required pixel dimensions. It validates typical ranges and alerts you if values fall outside normal specifications.

## Common DPI Standards

- **300 DPI**: Professional photography, fine art printing, and high-quality color publications
- **150 DPI**: Standard offset printing, everyday documents, and brochures
- **72 DPI**: Web graphics and screen display
- **96 DPI**: Standard Windows screen resolution

## Important Notes

This calculator provides reference values based on industry standards. For critical professional work—large format printing, fine art reproduction, or archival materials—consult with a print specialist. Values are current to 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.q: What DPI do I need for printing?
faq.a: 300 DPI is the standard for professional photo printing and publications. Use 150 DPI for everyday documents and brochures. For large format banners viewed from a distance, 150 DPI is sufficient.

faq.q: What's the difference between DPI and pixels?
faq.a: Pixels are individual dots in a digital image file. DPI (dots per inch) is the print resolution that determines how many pixels print per inch of paper. The formula is: pixels = inches × DPI.

faq.q: Can I fix a low-resolution image by increasing DPI?
faq.a: No. Increasing DPI in software won't improve image quality. If your original image is 72 DPI, changing it to 300 DPI just stretches the same pixels further apart, resulting in blurry output.

faq.q: What resolution do I need for large banners?
faq.a: Large banners viewed from a distance need only 150 DPI. Materials viewed up close—business cards, brochures, photographs—should use 300 DPI for sharp text and images.

faq.q: Should I use CMYK or RGB for printing?
faq.a: Use CMYK (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key/Black) for all print materials. RGB is for screens only. Convert to CMYK early in your workflow to avoid color shifts.

faq.q: Are vector formats better for printing?
faq.a: Yes. Scalable vector formats like PDF and SVG are ideal for logos, text, and line art—they remain sharp at any size. Use raster formats (JPEG, PNG) only for photographs.
Very accurate. This calculator uses the standard DPI formula validated by print industry standards. Results are reliable for planning, though consult a print professional for mission-critical work.

faq.q: Is my data stored when I use this calculator?
faq.a: No. All calculations happen instantly in your browser. Your data is never sent to a server or stored anywhere.

faq.q: I have a camera with 20 megapixels—what print size is that?
faq.a: Use this calculator's megapixel output to understand your image's dimensions. Most cameras use a 3:2 or 4:3 aspect ratio. Work backward from pixels to find your maximum print size at your target DPI.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data update required
